full_response:
Step-by-step solution

1.  Kinematics of the rotating platform  
   •  Let the tangential speed of the point at radius D be  
      v = 0.8 c.  
   •  The associated Lorentz factor is  
      γ = 1/√(1 – v²/c²) = 1/√(1 – 0.64) = 1/0.6 = 5/3.

2.  What length‐contraction affects  
   •  Segments oriented tangentially (around the circumference) are parallel to the direction of motion and are Lorentz-contracted in the inertial (lab) frame.  
   •  Segments oriented radially are perpendicular to the motion; no Lorentz contraction acts on their lengths.

3.  Radial distance measured by a builder  
   The builder is at rest with every point on the spoke that lies at the same radius.  
   Therefore the proper length of the spoke from the builder’s position to the hub is the same as its length in the lab frame:  

     ℓ_radial = D.  

4.  Circumference measured by a builder  
   •  Picture the builder laying down a stock of identical metre-sticks that are co-moving with him, all aligned tangentially, snug end-to-end around the circle.  
   •  Each of those sticks has its rest (proper) length L₀.  In the lab frame the same stick looks shorter by the factor γ:  

     L_lab = L₀/γ.  

   •  The lab-frame circumference of the circle is the geometric one, C_lab = 2πD.  
   •  Number of sticks required, as counted by the builder:

     N = C_lab / L_lab = (2πD) / (L₀/γ) = γ (2πD)/L₀.  

   •  Multiplying that number by the proper length of each stick gives the circumference that the builder actually measures (proper length around the rim):

     C_builder = N L₀ = γ (2πD).  

5.  Numerical value for v = 0.8 c  
   γ = 5/3 ⇒  

     C_builder = (5/3) · 2πD = (10π/3) D ≈ 1.67 × (2πD).

6.  Comparison  
   The circumference the builders find is larger than the Euclidean value 2πD by the factor γ.  Equivalently, the ratio  

     C_builder / D = 2πγ,

   so the “effective π” they would infer is π_eff = γπ > π, revealing a non-Euclidean spatial geometry on the rotating disk.

\boxed{C_{\text{measured}}=\gamma\,2\pi D=\frac{5}{3}\,2\pi D=\frac{10\pi}{3}\,D}

Confidence: 78%
